questions.question: Show that for any positive integer $n$, the expression $n^3 - n$ is always divisible by $6$.
questions.answer: The expression $n^3 - n$ can be factored as $(n-1)n(n+1)$, which represents the product of three consecutive integers. In any set of three consecutive integers, at least one must be a multiple of $2$ and exactly one must be a multiple of $3$. Since $2$ and $3$ are coprime, their product $6$ must divide the expression.

steps: Observe that $(n-1), n, (n+1)$ are three consecutive integers.
State the property that in any two consecutive integers, one must be even (divisible by $2$).
State the property that in any three consecutive integers, one must be a multiple of $3$.
Conclude that the product must be divisible by both $2$ and $3$.
Since $\text{gcd}(2, 3) = 1$, the product is divisible by $2 \times 3 = 6$.
solution.derivation: $n^3 - n = n(n^2 - 1) = (n-1)n(n+1) \\ \text{Let } P = (n-1)n(n+1) \\ \text{Since } (n-1), n, (n+1) \text{ are consecutive integers, at least one is divisible by } 2, \text{ so } 2 \mid P \\ \text{Also, one of them must be divisible by } 3, \text{ so } 3 \mid P \\ \text{Since } 2 \text{ and } 3 \text{ are coprime, } \text{lcm}(2, 3) = 6 \mid P \\ \therefore n^3 - n \text{ is divisible by } 6 \text{ for all } n \in \mathbb{N}$
